coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots as a multifunctional platform for real-time corrosion monitoring and inhibition
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zheng
Jia, Ruonan
Jian, Yang
Chen, Feng
Yan, Guilong
He, Dongxu
coredata.dc:description: The occurrence of corrosion can’t be completely eliminated. To prevent the failure of structural materials, corrosion has to be easily identified, visualized and detected at early-stage, in order to intervene at the point where it is necessary. In this view, fluorescent N-doped CDs have been synthesized to synergistically inhibit and monitor corrosion of iron. The as-prepared CDs were fabricated by simple solvothermal process using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia as dopant. It is found that the active groups on the surface of the CDs promote the formation of adsorption film of CDs on iron plates and effective fluorescence emission. As a result, the CDs could readily inhibit the corrosion of iron and exhibit an inhibition efficiency of 88 % at 180 mg/L. As the degree of corrosion increases, the CDs-adsorption film can sensitively, visually and effectively respond to released Fe3+ due to the complexation between CDs and Fe3+. Due to this dual function of CDs for corrosion monitoring and inhibition, they can readily trace early-stage corrosion and prevent its development. This is a synergistic strategy to prevent critical engineering failures and to ensure the safety of structural metal materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron corrosion
Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Research start-up funds of Chengdu University of Technology , China (Grant No. 10201908228 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis process of the CDs. (b) 3D fluorescence spectroscopy of the CDs.
(a) TEM image of the CDs; (b) Particle size distribution of the CDs determined from TEM; (c) FTIR spectrum, (d) XRD pattern, (e) Raman spectrum and (f) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.
(a) OCP-time curve; (b) polarization curves for iron plates in 1 M HCl containing different concentrations of CDs.
EIS plot of iron plates in 1 M HCl with different concentrations of CDs (a) Nyquist plot; (b) and (c) Bode plot; (d) Equivalent circuit model.

The SEM images and EDS spectra of the iron plates after immersion in 1 M HCl in the absence (a, b) and presence of CDs (c, d); The 3D AFM images of the iron plates immersed in 1 M HCl in the absence (e) and presence of CDs (f).

(a) The illustration of the responsiveness of CDs towards Fe3+; (b) The fluorescence spectra of CDs upon addition of corrosion solutions after the iron plates have been immersed in corrosion medium (0.1 M HCl) for different times (5–120 min). (c) The fluorescence intensity change ratio at 450 nm (I/I0) of CDs versus immersion time of iron plates in 0.1 M HCl. I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm before and after the addition of corrosion solutions. Inset: images of CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light before and after adding corrosion solutions.

(a) The illustration of the proposed corrosion imaging process based on CDs. (b) CLSM images of iron plates coated with CDs after immersion in 0.1 M HCl for 0, 5, 10 and 15 min (c) Images of iron plates coated with CDs after immersion in 0.1 M HCl for 0, 5 and 10 min.

(a) Corrosion rates of iron plates before and after the addition of CDs and the inhibition efficiency of CDs obtained from the weight loss measurement. (b) The weight loss ratio (W0-W)/W0 of iron plates and fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0-I)/I0 of CDs versus immersion time of iron plates in 0.1 M HCl.
The proposed mechanism of as-prepared CDs for synergistic corrosion inhibition and monitoring of iron.
Electrochemical polarization parameters for iron plates in 1 M HCl containing different concentrations of CDs.
The inhibition efficiency comparisons with some other corrosion inhibitors.
The electrochemical fitting parameters obtained from EIS data for iron plates in 1 M HCl containing different concentrations of CDs.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots as a multifunctional platform for real-time corrosion monitoring and inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The occurrence of corrosion can’t be completely eliminated. To prevent the failure of structural materials, corrosion has to be easily identified, visualized and detected at early-stage, in order to intervene at the point where it is necessary. In this view, fluorescent N-doped CDs have been synthesized to synergistically inhibit and monitor corrosion of iron. The as-prepared CDs were fabricated by simple solvothermal process using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia as dopant. It is found that the active groups on the surface of the CDs promote the formation of adsorption film of CDs on iron plates and effective fluorescence emission. As a result, the CDs could readily inhibit the corrosion of iron and exhibit an inhibition efficiency of 88 % at 180 mg/L. As the degree of corrosion increases, the CDs-adsorption film can sensitively, visually and effectively respond to released Fe3+ due to the complexation between CDs and Fe3+. Due to this dual function of CDs for corrosion monitoring and inhibition, they can readily trace early-stage corrosion and prevent its development. This is a synergistic strategy to prevent critical engineering failures and to ensure the safety of structural metal materials.

This dual function of CDs, for corrosion monitoring and inhibition, creates an integrated approach for corrosion assessment and protection to prevent corrosion-related failures.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data are included in this Article and its Supplementary Information files.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion could induce enormous destruction and damage to metal-based equipment, leading to great threat to the safety of human life and properties [1,2]. Thus, the anticorrosion technologies of metals have been widely investigated in the industry due to the widespread Cl- containing media and inorganic acids used for industrial pickling [3–5]. The employment of corrosion inhibitors is a common strategy to protect metals from corrosion. A variety of corrosion inhibitors with high inhibition efficiency have been developed, such as chromate, mercury salts and heteroatoms-containing compounds [6,7]. However, due to the influence of the external environment, the inhibition of corrosion is not once and for all. There is no inhibitor which is effective enough to completely stop the development of corrosion. It requires regular assessment. Thus, to ensure effective inhibition of corrosion, the monitoring of corrosion degree is of great significance as well, which makes it possible to intervene at the point where it is necessary to prevent corrosion development [8]. However, a corrosion inhibition system with dual functions, i.e., corrosion inhibition and monitoring, has not been widely investigated. Moreover, most of the conventional corrosion inhibitors are expensive and toxic for human health and ecological environment [9–11]. Hence, the urgency of our investigation is to fabricate an eco-friendly material with low cost, which is expected to synergistically inhibit corrosion and monitor corrosion degree.

Due to the good water solubility, simple preparation process, low toxicity and environmental friendliness, carbon dots（CDs）have attracted increasing attention [12–14]. Recently, CDs exhibited great potential for research in the anti-corrosion field [15–19]. Cui et al. synthesized N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for the inhibition of Q235 carbon steel in the presence of 1 M HCl. The results demonstrated that the inhibition efficiency reached up to 97 % [20]. Using citric acid as raw material, Ye et al. revealed the relationship between anticorrosion efficiency of CDs and the synthetic conditions of CDs. The highest inhibition efficiency was calculated to be 90 % [21]. Zhang et al. prepared a kind of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs, which contained 17 % N and 19 % S. The corrosion inhibition tests of CDs at 298–318 K in 0.5 M H2SO4 demonstrated that the CDs could still maintain good inhibition performance at high temperature [22]. But, aside from their excellent corrosion inhibition performance, CDs are fascinating candidates for cell imaging and fluorescent sensing [23,24]. Inspired by this, we assumed that CDs which can be readily respond to corrosion may be used as smart tag to reveal the corrosion degree. Furthermore, through rational molecular design, CDs with excellent corrosion inhibition and fluorescent sensing properties can be developed. The double-function corrosion monitoring and inhibition system helps to real-time detect the degree of corrosion, provide early warning and prevent corrosion development.

In this study, CDs with bright fluorescence were prepared by simple solvothermal process using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia as dopant. It is found that the active groups on the surface of the CDs promote the formation of adsorption film of CDs on iron plate and effective fluorescence emission. As a result, the CDs could readily inhibit the corrosion of iron and exhibit an inhibition efficiency of 88 % at 180 mg/L. As the degree of corrosion increases, the CDs-adsorption film can sensitively, visually and effectively respond to released Fe3+ due to the complexation between CDs and Fe3+. This dual function of CDs, for corrosion monitoring and inhibition, creates an integrated approach for corrosion assessment and protection to prevent corrosion-related failures.

Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR), anhydrous citric acid (CA, 99.5 %) and ammonia were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Ltd. AR-grade 37 wt % hydrochloric acid and 30 % hydrogen peroxide were purchased from KESHI Chemicals Ltd. The corrosion solution was prepared by diluting hydrochloric acid with deionized water. The iron plates were purchased from Shenyun Co., Ltd. Before use, the iron plates were polished with abrasive paper and washed through ethanol. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized according to our previous work [25]. Anhydrous citric acid (1.0 g) was dissolved in 15 mL DMF, and then the solution was kept stirring while 5 mL ammonia solution was added drop by drop. Subsequently, the obtained mixture was transferred into 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves. After heating at 180 °C for 12 h, the dark brown suspension of CDs was obtained. The crude product was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rad/min) for 20 min to remove large or agglomerated particles.

The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of CDs were recorded on HITACHI F-320 fluorescence spectrometer. The morphology of the CDs was investigated by TEM (JEM-2100 F). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested by AXIS ULTRA DLD from Kratos Analytical Ltd. The FTIR spectra were measured by VERTEX 70 from Bruker Corporation. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs was conducted by RigakuSmart Lab 9Kw from Rigaku Corporation. The AFM images of the iron plates after corrosion was carried out by BRUKER Dimension Icon. The Lambda 35 from PerkinElmer was employed to investigate the UV–vis absorption of CDs in the range of 250–800 nm. The Raman spectrum was recorded by Renishaw inVia. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were provided with a Phenom Pharos field emission SEM instrument. And the fluorescence of CDs was visualized by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) from Nikon Corporation.

Polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were executed using a CHI660E electrochemical station with a conventional three-electrode system to evaluate the anti-corrosion performance of CDs. The working electrode, auxiliary electrode and reference electrode were iron plate, platinum plate and saturated calomel electrode, respectively. For each test, the working electrode was immersed in the electrolyte solution for 30 min to achieve a stable open circuit potential (OCP). Polarization curves were measured in the potential range of ± 250 mV and the scanning rate was 1 mV/s. The EIS was measured at a frequency range of 100 kHz ~0.1 Hz with 10 mV amplitude. Then the obtained EIS data was fitted using the Zsimpwin 3.21 software. Before test, the working electrode specimen (3.0 cm × 1.5 cm) was cut from the fresh iron plate. The corresponding working area was 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm. It was abraded with the SiC abrasive papers of 600 grit and it was ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water and dried at room temperature. Hydrochloric acid (37 wt %) was diluted with deionized water to prepare 1 M HCl solution. For corrosion inhibition tests, 1 M HCl solution was used with different concentrations of CDs (0, 30, 60, 120, 180 mg/L). The experiment was performed at the room temperature and repeated three times under the same experimental conditions to ensure good reproducibility.

The specimens (20 mm × 15 mm × 0.3 mm) were cut from fresh iron plates. First, the specimens were washed with deionized water and ethanol in sequence. And the specimen was weighed three times on an analytical balance after drying. Then, the specimens were immersed into 1 M HCl solutions in the absence and presence of various concentrations of CDs. The specimens were removed from the corrosion solution after 24 h and cleaned with deionized water. Finally, the specimens were dried at room temperature and weighed three times to obtain the average weight after corrosion. Thus, the corrosion rates (v, g m−2h−1) for each specimen could be obtained as follows [26]:
(3)
v =
W
0
−
W
1
St
where W0 and W1 present the mass (g) of specimen before and after immersion, S is the specimen area (m2) immersed in the solution, and t is the immersion time (h).

The inhibition efficiency (η) could be calculated as follows:
(4)
η
%
=
v
0
− v
v
0
× 100
V0 and V are the corrosion rates of the specimens in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively.

100 µL of CDs stock solution was diluted 100 times with deionized water for fluorescence test. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured at 365 nm excitation wavelength. The iron plates (1.5 cm × 2 cm) were immersed in 0.1 M HCl for different times and the corrosion solutions were sampled for corrosion detection. Before fluorescence detection, 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution was added to oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+. The reactions were illustrated in the Supporting Information. Besides, the fluorescence response of CDs towards 0.1 M HCl solution with hydrogen peroxide was measured as control group.

The iron plates in rectangle were immersed in 0.1 M HCl solution in the presence of CDs. After immersion for 0, 5, 10, and 15 min, 30 % hydrogen peroxide was added to oxidize the Fe2+ into Fe3+. Then the iron plates covered with CDs were observed under CLSM. The excitation wavelength was 405 nm. The other iron plates in bulb and diamond shapes were immersed in 0.1 M HCl solution for corrosion test. After a period of time (0, 5, 10 min), the iron plates were removed from the corrosive medium and the Fe2+ was oxidized into Fe3+ by dropping 30 % hydrogen peroxide on the surface of the iron plate. Then, the iron plates were coated with CDs and observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The N-doped carbon dots were prepared by simple solvothermal process, using citric acid as the carbon precursor and ammonia as the dopant, as shown in Fig. 1a. The as-prepared CDs exhibited bright fluorescence under the UV lamp. Fluorescence spectrometer was used to analyze the fluorescence characterization of CDs. As seen in Fig. 1b, CDs showed an emission band from 450 to 500 nm, which exhibited blue light emission upon excitation. It can be seen that the emission peak of CDs gradually shifted from 450 to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength changed from 365 to 425 nm, demonstrating the excitation dependent emission properties of CDs [27]. Considering the extensive use of 365 nm light source for in situ fluorescence observation, the fluorescence of CDs was observed and measured under 365 nm excitation.

TEM was used to characterize the morphology of the CDs. It is obvious that the CDs were well dispersed in the suspension without aggregation as shown in Fig. 2a. The CDs presented a uniform spherical shape. The particle sizes of the CDs were determined to be 2–8 nm with an average particle size of 5.3 nm, as seen in Fig. 2b. The chemical structure of the as-prepared CDs was confirmed by the FTIR spectrum. In Fig. 2c, there are several characteristic peaks at around 2925 cm−1, 1637 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1, which indicated the C-H, CC and C-C stretching vibration, respectively [28,29]. The stretching vibrations of O-H (3430 cm−1) and CO (1706 cm−1) were clearly observed, which can be attributed to the carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups of CDs [30]. The appearance of absorption peaks at 1405 cm−1 can be ascribed to C-N group, implying that nitrogen was successfully doped. The XRD was further applied to determine the structure of CDs. As seen in Fig. 2d, the pattern showed a broad peak at 20° and no sharp peak. It revealed that the amorphous graphite structures were probably the dominant part of the CDs [31]. To confirm the results, the Raman spectrum of CDs was conducted as shown in Fig. 2e. The Raman spectrum of CDs exhibited two peaks at approximately 1560 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1, which corresponded to the G band and D band, respectively. The G band demonstrated the existence of graphitic sp2 hybridization and the D band indicated the disordered structure of CDs [32]. It is obvious that the Raman spectrum is consistent with the result of XRD test. The optical properties of CDs were also explored. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 2f. There are two absorption peaks at 260 and 340 nm, which could be attributed to the π-π*and n-π*transitions of CC, and CO bonds [33]. It demonstrated the conjugated molecular structure of CDs which may be responsible for the fluorescence of CDs.

The chemical compositions and bonds have great impact on the adsorption behavior of CDs towards metal plates and their fluorescence properties. Thus, XPS was utilized to further check the chemical structures of the surface of CDs. Fig. 3a shows the full spectrum of CDs. It can be seen that there are three peaks at 284.8, 399.7, and 531.9 eV respectively, which were considered to be the peaks of C, N, and O. Meanwhile, the quantitative analysis revealed that the elemental composition of the CDs is C (50.88 %), N (5.56 %), and O (43.57 %). Moreover, a detailed investigation of CDs was performed. As shown in Fig. 3b, the C 1 s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks, which are C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.08 eV), and CO/CN (288.62 eV), respectively [34]. The N 1 s spectrum as seen in Fig. 3c displayed two peaks at 399.51 eV and 400.12 eV, which can be ascribed to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [35]. For O 1 s in Fig. 3d, the deconvoluted spectrum exhibited two peaks at 531.81, and 533.25 eV, which could be attributed to C-O and CO, respectively [18,36]. The XPS results showed good correspondence with FTIR analysis. All these findings indicated the abundance of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs.

The open-circuit potential (OCP) was employed to understand the deposition process of CDs on the iron surface. As shown in Fig. 4a, the OCP-time curves of the iron plates in pure HCl and CDs solutions were recorded. It can be seen that the OCP followed the same trend. All the OCP curves became flat with prolonged time, indicating that the corrosion system gradually became stable. With an increase in the concentration of CDs, the OCP values moved to the positive direction at the same condition. The elevation of the value of the corrosion potentials led to the decrease of the corrosion reaction. It demonstrated that the adsorption of CDs on the electrode may occur and prevent the contact between the iron plate and the corrosion solution. Therefore, the corrosion was suppressed as the CDs’ concentration increased.

The potentiodynamic polarization technique was used to study the inhibition performance of CDs. The polarization curves of the iron plates in 1 M HCl containing different concentrations of CDs are presented in Fig. 4b. The corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), anodic and cathodic Tafel slope (βa, βc) were obtained by the classical extrapolation method, as seen in Table 1. The degree of surface coverage (θ) and inhibition efficiency (η) values are calculated from the icorr values using the following equations [26].
(3)
Ɵ =
i
corr , 0
−
i
corr
i
corr
(4)
η
%
=
i
corr , 0
−
i
corr
i
corr
× 100
where icorr,0 and icorr represent the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, respectively. It can be found that the Ecorr gradually shifted from − 474.7 to − 453.7 mV in the positive direction as the CDs’ concentration increased. But the variation of Ecorr was less than 30 mV [37]. Meanwhile, the polarization curves showed that both the cathode and anode reaction shifted to a lower icorr after CDs were added as corrosion inhibitor. The changes in Ecorr and icorr indicated that the CDs were the mixed-type corrosion inhibitor [38]. It is clear that the icorr exhibited a downtrend along with the increase of the concentration of CDs. The icorr of iron plate in pure HCl was 146.6 × 10−5 A/cm2. The icorr decreased to 55.9 × 10−5 A/cm2 upon addition of 30 mg/L CDs. Then, the icorr of the iron plate dropped to 20.4 × 10−5 A/cm2 with 180 mg/L CDs. These trends demonstrated the effective corrosion inhibition of CDs. The inhibition can be enhanced with the increase of the concentration of CDs. In addition, with the addition of CDs, icorr gradually reduced as the concentration of CDs increased. The η values were 67.5 %, 66.4 %, 81.3 % and 87.5 % respectively when 30, 60, 120, 180 mg/L CDs were added as corrosion inhibitor. The results implied CDs have excellent corrosion inhibition performance. Besides, the standard deviations (SD) of three independent measurements were calculated. The low value shows the experimental data are reliable. Compared with inhibition efficiency calculated from polarization curves measurements from some previous studies, the inhibition efficiency of CDs was relatively higher under similar conditions, as seen in Table 2.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out to further investigate the corrosion inhibition behavior of iron plates in 1 M HCl with or without different concentrations of CDs. Nyquist image and Bode diagrams were acquired from EIS test. The equivalent circuit model was built to fit the EIS results. The Nyquist image showed the capacitive semicircle at high frequencies and the inductive arc at low frequencies, as shown in Fig. 5a. All the Nyquist images exhibited similar patterns, but the diameter of the capacitive loop increased significantly with the addition of the CDs. And it showed an ascend trend with the increase of CDs concentration [46]. The presence of capacitance semicircles at high frequencies was considered to be related to charge transfer and double-layer capacitance [47]. Thus, the larger diameter indicated that the CDs adsorption film has been readily formed on the surface of the iron plates. With increased concentration of CDs, the absorbed film became dense and compact. During this process, the charge transfer process was suppressed. Moreover, the impedance modulus |Z| at low frequency was verified to be inversely proportional to the corrosion rate of electrodes. As shown in Fig. 5b, it is obvious that adding CDs as corrosion inhibitor could result in the increase of |Z|0.1 Hz. The values exhibited a rising trend with the increase in the concentration of CDs. Specifically, the |Z|0.1 Hz of the iron plate in pure HCl was 13.5 Ω cm2 and the |Z|0.1 Hz reached to 64.7 Ω cm2 after 180 mg/L CDs was added. Similarly, the phase angle plot showed that the height of the peak increased with increased concentration of CDs as seen in Fig. 5c. These results affirmed the formation of adsorption film of CDs on the surface of the iron plates and the relatively strong interaction between CDs and iron plates, on the other hand.

To get a deep insight into the corrosion process, the equivalent circuit model was acquired and the relevant fitting parameters are listed in Table 3. In the electrical equivalent circuits as illustrated in Fig. 5d, the RS, RP, CPEdl represent the solution resistance, polarisation resistance, constant phase angle element, respectively. L is the inductance and RL is the corresponding resistance. Due to the influence of the inhibitor adsorption, rough surface or other energy dissipation, CPEdl is used to replace an ideal capacitance [39]. As shown in Table 3, the value of RS changed slightly before and after the corrosion inhibitor was added. It can be found that the value of RP increased significantly after the addition of CDs. As the concentration of CDs increased, the value of RP increased correspondingly, revealing that the adsorbed film became more valid and the corrosion resistance improved. The best inhibition effect was obtained at 180 mg/L CDs and the RP value was 69.3 Ω cm2. When the CDs particles were adsorbed on the surface, the layer of water molecules between the metal electrode and the solution can be readily replaced by a layer of corrosion inhibitor particles. As the dielectric constant of water molecules is much larger than that of corrosion inhibitor particles, the CPEdl decreased after the introduction of CDs. The results of EIS verified that CDs as corrosion inhibitor can effectively protect iron plates in 1 M HCl. The excellent corrosion inhibition performance of CDs are consistent with the potentiodynamic polarization measurement.

The surface morphology of the iron plates immersed in 1 M HCl with or without CDs was investigated by SEM and AFM, as shown in Fig. 6. In Fig. 6a, it can be seen that the unprotected iron plate was severely corroded with large cracks and pits after being immersed in 1 M HCl for 24 h. There were obvious corrosion products owing to the strong acidity of the corrosion solution. However, in the presence of 180 mg/L CDs, the iron plate was protected from corrosion, and the surface was found to be relatively smooth with a few shallow pits as seen in Fig. 6c. The EDS spectra were further employed to analyze the elemental composition on the iron plate surface. As shown in Fig. 6b and Fig. 6d, three kinds of elements (Fe, C, O) were observed on the surface of the iron plates. Compared with the unprotected iron plates, the intensity of C peak increased and the intensity of Fe peak decreased in the protected iron plates. Based on the C-rich feature of CDs, these results suggested that CDs-adsorbed film was formed on the iron plate. This barrier effectively prevented iron from corrosion attack, leading to the decrease of Fe peak. Furthermore, AFM was performed to show the surface roughness of the iron plates after corrosion tests. Figs. 6e and 6f show the 3D morphology of the iron plates immersed in 1 M HCl with or without CDs. It is obvious that the uninhibited surface exhibited larger fluctuation with deep pits compared to the samples protected with CDs. The height profile of the iron plates made along the marked line also showed the fluctuation of the surface. As seen in Fig. S1 and S2, the unprotected surface had large fluctuations, while the protected surface had few fluctuations. The average roughness (Ra) of the iron plates without and with CDs was calculated to be 42.4 nm and 27.2 nm, respectively, indicating that CDs can remarkably reduce the corrosion rates of the iron plate. These findings provided strong support for electrochemical results and further confirmed the formation of the CDs-adsorbed film on iron plates.

The adsorption process of CDs on the surface of the iron plate was investigated by the Langmuir isotherm model. The adsorption isotherm was calculated based on previous study [48,49]:
(5)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
ads
C
where θ, C, and Kads were the surface coverage, inhibitor concentration and adsorption equilibrium constant, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, the R- square was 0.99194 of the linear fitting result of the iron plate in HCl solutions. This shows that the adsorption process of CDs was in good accordance with Langmuir adsorption model. Also, the adsorption free energy (ΔG0 ads) was calculated [50]:
(6)
K
ads
=
1
C
H 2 O
exp
(
− Δ
G
ads
0
RT
)
where R and T are the molar gas constant and the absolute temperature, respectively. It can be seen that the Kads value of the iron plate in HCl was calculated to be 62.8 L/g and the ΔG0 ads value was estimated to be − 27.37 kJ/mol, respectively. Moreover, the value of ΔG0 ads can be used to speculate the type of CDs adsorbed on the surface of the iron plates. As reported, it is considered to be physical interaction between the as-prepared CDs and the iron plate if the ΔG0 ads is higher than − 20 kJ/mol [51]. It tends to be chemical adsorption if the ΔG0 ads is lower than − 40 kJ/mol [52]. Thus, according to the calculation, the adsorption mechanism of as-prepared CDs on the interface was physicochemical interaction. In this process, CDs may be first adsorbed onto the iron surface via electrostatic interaction. Then, chemisorption of CDs occurred based on the donor-acceptor interaction.

The feasibility and effectiveness of CDs as corrosion inhibitor for iron have been profoundly discussed. However, it is impossible to completely eliminate the occurrence of corrosion in the industry actually. To prevent the failure of structural materials, corrosion has to be easily identified, visualized and detected at early-stage, in order to intervene at the point where it is necessary. In this view, fluorescent N-doped CDs have been synthesized to synergistically inhibit and monitor corrosion of iron. As shown in Fig. 7a, 0.1 M HCl was used as corrosion medium to illustrate the performance of the as-prepared CDs for the detection of early-stage corrosion which is easily-ignored in most case. It can be seen that the CDs are able to rapidly, sensitively and selectively respond to Fe3+. The bright blue fluorescence emitted by the CDs was gradually quenched with increase in the concentration of Fe3+. The detailed response mechanism is illustrated in our previous work. In this case, iron plates which have been exposed to 0.1 M HCl can readily dissociate Fe2+ into the environment according to the electrochemical process of iron corrosion. Then the concentration of Fe2+ is regarded as an indicator of the development of corrosion process. Hydrogen peroxide was used to transform Fe2+ into Fe3+ for fluorescent sensing. The corrosion monitoring performance of CDs was assessed as follows. First, the iron plates were immersed in 0.1 M HCl and a certain amount of corrosion solution was sampled at specific time intervals. Then the fluorescence intensity of CDs was recorded upon addition of the corrosion solutions. As shown in Fig. 7b, it is obvious that all groups exhibited a characteristic emission band at around 450 nm. But the intensity of the fluorescence significantly decreased with increase in the immersion time of iron plates in 0.1 M HCl. The decrease in the intensity indicates an increase in the concentration of Fe3+ in the solution, as well as an enhanced corrosion state of iron plates. The plot of the fluorescence intensity change ratio at 450 nm (I/I0) versus immersion time of the iron plate is illustrated in Fig. 7c. It can be clearly seen that the fluorescence of CDs was reduced quickly up to half of the original when the iron plates were immersed in HCl solution for 10 min. Then the value of I/I0 dropped by more than 90 % when the iron plates were corroded in HCl solution for 120 min. The images of the CDs before and after adding different concentrations of corrosion solutions are given in Fig. 7c. It can be seen that the color of the CDs under the visible light changed slightly, but the fluorescence was quenched obviously under the UV light. The fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon was easily recognized by the observer, which provides a simple method for corrosion detection. All of the fluorescence sensing results demonstrated not only the practical use of CDs for early-stage corrosion monitoring but also the dual test modes of instrument and naked-eyes.

The corrosion monitoring process using CDs was proposed in Fig. 8a. Once the iron plates are contaminated by acidic solution such as HCl, a trace of corrosion products is formed in these regions. Then the as-prepared CDs as eco-friendly and low-cost fluorescent sensors can be applied for corrosion imaging. Due to the superior responsiveness of CDs, the unnoticeable corrosion was captured instantly. CLSM was further employed to investigate the in situ response process of CDs on the surface of the iron plate. As shown in Fig. 8b, the iron plate showed strong fluorescence emission of CDs before corrosion happened. After immersion in 0.1 M HCl for 5 min, the fluorescence of the CDs on the surface of the iron plate began to diminish. After 10 min, the fluorescence was almost quenched. Upon immersion for 15 min, the fluorescence quenched completely. Furthermore, the iron plates were fabricated into specific shapes for macroscopic observation of fluorescence changes after corrosion. As shown in Fig. 8c, hydrogen peroxide and CDs were dropped in sequence on the surface of the corroded iron plates. Then the iron plates coated with CDs were observed under 365 nm UV light. It can be seen that compared with the control groups the fluorescence intensity of the iron plates after 5 min corrosion was significantly quenched. After10 min, the fluorescence quenching was obvious enough to be detected by the naked eyes. The phenomena are consistent with the results observed from CLSM tests. They demonstrated that both macroscopic and microscopic changes in fluorescence can be used as signals to trace the corrosion area and detect the extent of corrosion.

Fig. 9 summarizes the corrosion inhibition and monitoring performance of CDs for iron plates. Fig. 9a shows the corrosion rates of iron plates before and after the addition of CDs and the inhibition efficiency of different concentrations of CDs. It can be seen that the corrosion rate sharply reduced from 21.9 g m−2 h−1 to 4.9 g m−2 h−1 with 30 mg/L CDs as corrosion inhibitor. Then, it decreased further as the concentration of CDs increased, reaching 2.4 g m−2 h−1 at 180 mg/L. Correspondingly, the corrosion inhibition efficiency increased from 77.5 % for 30 mg/L to 89.3 % for 180 mg/L. These results agreed with the electrochemical measurements, implying the excellent corrosion inhibition ability of the as-prepared CDs. Meanwhile, the relationship between the corrosion process of iron plates and the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs was illustrated to verify the reliability of CDs for corrosion monitoring. As shown in Fig. 9b, it can be found that the weight loss rate of the iron plates and the fluorescence change ratio exhibited similar upward trend during the electrochemical corrosion process. The weight loss rate increased with the increase of immersion time, and the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs increased as well. It is the result of the iron dissolution in the corrosive medium causing the increase of Fe2+. In the presence of oxides such as O2 and hydrogen peroxide, Fe2+ was oxidized into Fe3+ which can be readily detected by the as-prepared CDs. Thus, due to the positive correlation between the corrosion extent of iron and the fluorescence quenching behavior of CDs, the availability of CDs for corrosion monitoring was fully proved.

The proposed mechanism of as-prepared CDs for synergistic corrosion inhibition and monitoring of iron is demonstrated in Fig. 10. First, due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of the CDs, there was physicochemical interaction between the CDs and the iron place, and the absorbed film was formed. With increase in CDs’ concentration, more dense and compact protected layer based on CDs can be obtained. The absorbed film can protect the iron plates from the corrosion of acidic medium. Thus, the corrosion of iron can be effectively inhibited. On the other hand, the surface of iron not covered with CDs-absorbed film suffers from serious electrochemical corrosion. Then, Fe2+ ions are released from the corrosion interface and will be oxidized into Fe3+ after exposure to oxides. The free Fe3+ ions are excellent indicators of corrosion degree for fluorescence imaging. According to the extent of fluorescence quenching, the degree of corrosion can be accurately, rapidly and real-time monitored by instruments and naked-eyes in situ.

In this study, CDs with bright fluorescence were prepared by simple solvothermal process using citric acid and ammonia as raw materials. Through electrochemical analysis, corrosion morphology and adsorption isotherm tests, the inhibition behavior of CDs for iron plates in 1 M HCl were investigated. It is revealed that the CDs could readily inhibit the corrosion of iron and exhibit an inhibition efficiency of 88 % at 180 mg/L. The adsorption of CDs on the surface of the iron plates involved both chemisorption and physisorption. Also, the as-prepared CDs can sensitively, visually and effectively respond to Fe3+. On this basis, the corrosion degree was successfully detected by the as-prepared CDs according to the fluorescence quenching signals by CLSM and naked-eyes. Weight loss measurements further verified the reliability and practicability of CDs as corrosion inhibitor and sensor. Due to their excellent corrosion inhibition and fluorescence imaging properties, the synthesized CDs provide an integrated approach for simultaneous corrosion inhibition and monitoring to prevent corrosion-related failures.

Zheng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ruonan Jia: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yang Jian: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Feng Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Guilong Yan: Methodology, Formal analysis. Dongxu He: Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research start-up funds of Chengdu University of Technology, China (Grant No. 10201908228).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129499.
